Galti se delivery class A marked table ka data customizing transports mein NAHI include hoga — even if data SM30 ke through maintained tha. Iska matlab QA mein maintained configuration transport ke through production tak nahi pohnchti — manually re-enter karni padti hai. Yeh late discover hone par go-live blocker hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The most impactful effect of wrong delivery class is on transport behavior. A table with delivery class C has its SM30-maintained data included in customizing transport requests — when functional consultants maintain configuration and release a transport, the data travels to the target system automatically. A table incorrectly marked as delivery class A will NOT have its data included in customizing transports — even if the data was maintained via SM30. This means configuration maintained in QA does not reach production via transport — it must be manually re-entered. This is a go-live blocker if discovered late.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Go-live issue: Custom pricing config table ZCFG_PRICE_RULES with delivery class A (should have been C). Functional consultant maintained 400 pricing rules in QA, transported to production. Transport arrived in production — table structure present, zero data rows. Emergency: changed delivery class to C in SE11, retransported structure. But data already not in transport. All 400 rules had to be manually entered in production overnight before go-live. 8-hour delay.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Table delivery class change karna after data is already maintained. Changing delivery class does not retroactively fix transport behavior for already-released transports. Catch delivery class mistakes early — before data entry starts in QA.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Verify delivery class before go-live:
SELECT tabname, contflag
  FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_cfg_tables)
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'ZCFG%'  " custom config tables
    AND as4local = 'A'.

LOOP AT lt_cfg_tables ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_tab>).
  IF <fs_tab>-contflag <> 'C'.  " should be C for config
    WRITE: / 'WARNING - wrong delivery class:',
             <fs_tab>-tabname, <fs_tab>-contflag.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does delivery class affect client copy behavior?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Client copy operation ke dauran, delivery class determine karta hai ki table data included hai ya nahi. Delivery class C (customizing) data customizing client copies mein included hota hai — naya client banane ke liye ideal jisme standard configuration pehle se setup ho. Delivery class A (application) data sirf full client copies mein included hota hai — jab aap saara business data including transactions replicate karna chahte ho. Delivery class L (temporary) data kabhi copy nahi hota — source client ke local hota hai. Delivery class G data cross-client hota hai aur saare clients mein bina copy ke visible hota hai. Scratch se QA client banate time, customizing copy mein C-class configuration include hota hai but no A-class transactional data — configuration ke saath clean client deta hai but no business data.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: During a client copy operation, the delivery class determines whether table data is included. Delivery class C (customizing) data is included in customizing client copies — ideal for creating a new client with standard configuration already set up. Delivery class A (application) data is included only in full client copies — when you want to replicate all business data including transactions. Delivery class L (temporary) data is never copied — it is local to the source client. Delivery class G data is cross-client and visible in all clients without copying. When creating a QA client from scratch, a customizing copy includes C-class configuration but no A-class transactional data — giving a clean client with configuration but no business data.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: New client setup: Created fresh QA client 210 as a customizing copy from client 200 (which had complete configuration). Customizing copy included all delivery class C tables — tax codes, payment terms, document types, company code settings. Application class A tables — open orders, deliveries, invoices — were not copied. QA client 210 had full configuration ready immediately without needing manual re-setup, and no contamination from production business data.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Custom config table delivery class L set karna thinking 'we do not want this data copied.' Better option: delivery class C with proper transport management. L class data is not transported AND not copied — it disappears in client copies and is lost after system refresh. Use L only for truly temporary technical data.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Delivery class impact on client copy:
" SCC4/SCCL: Client Copy transaction
" Copy profile: 'SAP_CUST' - copies C class, not A class
" Copy profile: 'SAP_ALL'  - copies A and C class data

" Check what client copy would include:
SELECT t000~mandt, t000~mtext, dd02l~tabname,
       dd02l~contflag
  FROM t000
  CROSS JOIN dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_check)
  WHERE dd02l~tabname = 'ZCFG_PAYMENT'
    AND dd02l~as4local = 'A'.
" contflag='C': included in customizing copy
" contflag='A': only in full copy
" contflag='L': never copied

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between delivery class A and delivery class C in practical terms?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Delivery class A application data ke liye hai — actual business transactions aur master data jo users daily operations mein create karte hain: sales orders, purchase orders, material movements, accounting documents, customer data. Yeh data system-specific hota hai — production mein live orders, QA mein test orders, development mein development data. Systems ke beech A-class data transport karna rarely hota hai aur usually wrong hai. Delivery class C customizing data ke liye hai — configuration jo system ko specific business ke liye specific tareeke se behave karwati hai: payment terms, pricing procedures, document types, tax codes. Yeh configuration intentionally systems ke across same hoti hai aur standard customizing transport requests ke through development se QA se production tak transport hoti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Delivery class A is for application data — the actual business transactions and master data that users create during daily operations: sales orders, purchase orders, material movements, accounting documents, customer data. This data is system-specific — production has live orders, QA has test orders, development has development data. Transporting A-class data between systems is rarely done and usually wrong. Delivery class C is for customizing data — the configuration that makes the system behave a specific way for a business: payment terms, pricing procedures, document types, tax codes. This configuration is intentionally the same across systems and is transported from development through QA to production via standard customizing transport requests.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Design decision on project: Two new custom tables needed. ZCUSTOMER_CONTRACTS stores contract agreements — users enter these, data unique per system. Delivery class A. ZCFG_CONTRACT_TYPES stores allowed contract types — functional consultant defines these in IMG-like setup. Delivery class C. Six months later: ZCFG_CONTRACT_TYPES transported cleanly from QA to production. ZCUSTOMER_CONTRACTS data stayed in each system independently. Correct design from day one saved multiple transport issues.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Delivery class decision during table design mein rush karna. Rule: ask two questions before setting delivery class: 1) Who maintains this data — user in daily work (A) or consultant in configuration (C)? 2) Should this data be same in all systems (C/G) or system-specific (A)? These two questions always lead to correct delivery class.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Example: Two related tables with different delivery classes

" Table ZCFG_REGION_CODES - Delivery Class C
" Config: valid region codes for the business
" Maintained by: functional consultant via SM30
" Transported: yes - same codes in all systems
" SE11: contflag = 'C'

" Table ZCUSTOMER_ADDR_HIST - Delivery Class A
" Application: actual customer address change history
" Created by: ABAP program when customer address changes
" Transported: no - production history stays in production
" SE11: contflag = 'A'

" Check in ABAP:
SELECT tabname, contflag
  FROM dd02l WHERE tabname IN
    ( 'ZCFG_REGION_CODES', 'ZCUSTOMER_ADDR_HIST' )
  AND as4local = 'A'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_result).

followupAnswerBank.question: What is delivery class L used for and what are the risks?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Delivery class L Local ya Temporary ke liye stand karta hai — in tables mein data transport nahi hota, client copies mein included nahi hota, aur SAP upgrades ke dauran delete ho sakta hai. L genuinely temporary technical purposes ke liye use karo: batch processing staging tables jahan data ek single job run ke andar create aur consume hota hai, session-specific temporary storage, ya development-only scratch tables. Risks: L-class table mein end hone wala koi bhi important data transport ke through production tak nahi pohnchega aur client copies ya upgrades mein lost ho jaayega. Agar table ko baad mein transport capability chahiye, delivery class change karni padti hai aur new transport release karni padti hai — jo abhi bhi table mein already mauzood data retroactively include nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Delivery class L stands for Local or Temporary — data in these tables is not transported, not included in client copies, and may be deleted during SAP upgrades. Use L for genuinely temporary technical purposes: batch processing staging tables where data is created and consumed within a single job run, session-specific temporary storage, or development-only scratch tables. The risks: any data considered important that ends up in an L-class table will not reach production via transport and will be lost in client copies or upgrades. If a table later needs transport capability, the delivery class must be changed and a new transport released — which still does not retroactively include data already in the table.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Correct use: Custom batch processing used table ZSTAGE_BATCH_PROC delivery class L to hold intermediate calculation results during long-running jobs. Data was created at job start and deleted at job end — purely temporary. No transport needed, no client copy needed. Correct use of L class. Wrong use (mistake on another project): custom configuration table accidentally given delivery class L. Config maintained in dev and QA — but not transported to production. Discovered at go-live.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: L class use karna thinking 'ye table important nahi hai.' If anyone will ever need the data in another system or after an upgrade — do not use L class. Only use L for data that is literally temporary and disposable after the current processing session.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Correct use of delivery class L:
" Temporary staging table for batch processing
" SE11: ZSTAGE_BATCH_PROC, Delivery Class: L
" Fields: MANDT, BATCH_ID (key), ZDATA fields

" Program creates temp data at start:
INSERT INTO zstage_batch_proc VALUES @ls_stage_record.

" Process data...
SELECT * FROM zstage_batch_proc
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_stage)
  WHERE batch_id = @lv_batch_id.

" Clean up at end (important for L class tables):
DELETE FROM zstage_batch_proc
  WHERE batch_id = @lv_batch_id.
" L class = temporary, always clean up after use

followupAnswerBank.question: How does delivery class affect SAP upgrade behavior?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SAP upgrades ke dauran, delivery class SAP standard tables ke liye data handling govern karta hai. Delivery class S aur E tables ke liye, SAP upgrade ke part ke roop mein updated data deliver karta hai — yeh SAP system aur control tables hain. Delivery class C aur A customer-maintained tables ke liye, SAP upgrade ke dauran existing data preserve karta hai — customer configuration aur application data overwrite nahi hote. Delivery class L data upgrades ke dauran clear ho sakta hai kyunki temporary consider hota hai. Custom Z tables ke liye, SAP upgrades unhe delivery class ke regardless touch nahi karte — yeh customer objects hain. Delivery class most matter karti hai SAP standard tables ke liye jahan aapne SAP-delivered content ke saath entries add ki hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: During SAP upgrades, delivery class governs data handling for SAP standard tables. For delivery class S and E tables, SAP delivers updated data as part of the upgrade — these are SAP system and control tables. For delivery class C and A customer-maintained tables, SAP preserves existing data during upgrade — customer configuration and application data are not overwritten. Delivery class L data may be cleared during upgrades as it is considered temporary. For custom Z tables, SAP upgrades do not touch them at all regardless of delivery class — they are customer objects. The delivery class matters most for SAP standard tables where you have added entries alongside SAP-delivered content.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Upgrade scenario: After applying an SAP Support Package, custom config entries in standard customizing tables (delivery class C like T042, T052) were preserved intact. Only SAP-delivered entries were updated. Custom application data in VBAK and related A-class tables — completely untouched by upgrade. Temporary L-class tables used for batch processing were cleared during the upgrade database sync — as designed. No surprises because delivery classes were correctly understood.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Upgrade ke baad panic karna ki 'SAP ne hamara config delete kar diya.' SAP upgrades preserve customer config (class C) aur application data (class A). If data disappears after upgrade — likely it was L class or there was a separate issue. Check delivery class first before blaming the upgrade.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check which tables SAP delivers content for:
SELECT tabname, contflag
  FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_sap_tables)
  WHERE contflag IN ('S', 'E', 'W')  " SAP-delivered classes
    AND tabname NOT LIKE 'Z%'         " exclude custom tables
    AND as4local = 'A'
  ORDER BY tabname.
" These tables get SAP content during upgrades
" Your Z tables (custom): never touched by SAP upgrades

" Verify custom table delivery class is A or C:
SELECT tabname, contflag
  FROM dd02l
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'Z%'
    AND as4local = 'A'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_ztables).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you decide the right delivery class when designing a custom table?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Delivery class selection decision framework: Pehle, identify karo ki data kaun create aur maintain karta hai. Agar ABAP programs ya business users ise daily operations ke dauran create karte hain — class A. Agar functional consultants implementation ke dauran ek baar setup karte hain aur yeh rarely change hota hai — class C. Agar yeh temporary processing data hai jo job run ke beyond survive nahi karna chahiye — class L. Doosre, poocho ki data saare systems ke across same hona chahiye. Configuration jo development, QA, aur production mein identical honi chahiye — class C. Data jo system-specific hai — class A. Teesre, transport requirements verify karo. Agar data ko QA se production transport karna chahiye — class C mandatory. Agar data per system rehta hai — class A. A aur C ke beech doubt mein, C choose karo — data transport na karna missed configuration manually re-enter karne se easier hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Delivery class selection decision framework: First, identify who creates and maintains the data. If ABAP programs or business users create it during daily operations — class A. If functional consultants set it up once during implementation and it rarely changes — class C. If it is temporary processing data that should not survive beyond a job run — class L. Second, ask if the data should be the same across all systems. Configuration that should be identical in development, QA, and production — class C. Data that is system-specific — class A. Third, verify transport requirements. If the data must be transported from QA to production — class C mandatory. If data stays per system — class A. When in doubt between A and C, choose C — it is easier to not transport data than to manually re-enter missed configuration.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Design session: Three new tables needed. ZCUSTOM_PLANT_CONFIG — stores configuration set by functional consultant via SM30, same in all systems. Decision: C. ZCUSTOM_PLANT_EVENTS — stores events logged by ABAP programs during plant operations, unique per system. Decision: A. ZCUSTOM_PLANT_STAGING — holds intermediate data during batch plant report processing, created and deleted within same job. Decision: L. All three decisions made in 5 minutes using the decision framework.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Default A lagana bina sochhe. Many developers default to A for all custom tables — then discover config is not transporting. Delivery class is a design decision, not an afterthought. Document it in table comments in SE11 why a specific delivery class was chosen.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Decision checklist as ABAP comment in custom table program:
*& Table: ZCUSTOM_PLANT_CONFIG
*& Delivery Class: C - Customizing
*& Reason: Maintained by functional consultant via SM30
*&          Same configuration needed in all systems
*&          Must transport from QA to Production
*&          SM30 maintenance dialog generated (TMG)

" Table: ZCUSTOM_PLANT_EVENTS
" Delivery Class: A - Application
" Reason: Created by ABAP program during operations
"          Per-system data, production events <> QA events
"          Not transported - system specific transactional data

" Check delivery class in ABAP at runtime:
SELECT SINGLE contflag FROM dd02l
  INTO @DATA(lv_class)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCUSTOM_PLANT_CONFIG'
    AND as4local = 'A'.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the relationship between delivery class and SM30 maintenance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SM30 Table Maintenance transaction hai aur dono delivery class A aur C tables mein data maintain karne ke liye use ho sakta hai — bashart ki Table Maintenance Generator run kiya gaya ho. However, critical behavior difference: jab functional consultant delivery class C table mein SM30 ke through data maintain karta hai aur customizing transport request release karta hai, uss table ka data automatically transport mein capture hota hai. SM30 ke through maintained delivery class A table ke liye, data customizing transport requests mein included NAHI hota. Yahi reason hai ki saari configuration tables jinhe development se QA se production tak travel karna hai delivery class C honi chahiye. SM30 ke through maintained delivery class A tables manual data transport ya data migration require karti hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SM30 is the Table Maintenance transaction and can be used to maintain data in both delivery class A and C tables — as long as Table Maintenance Generator has been run. However, the critical behavior difference: when a functional consultant maintains data in a delivery class C table via SM30 and releases a customizing transport request, that table's data is automatically captured in the transport. For a delivery class A table maintained via SM30, the data is NOT included in customizing transport requests. This is why all configuration tables that need to travel from development through QA to production must be delivery class C. Delivery class A tables maintained via SM30 require manual data transport or data migration.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Team training situation: Functional consultant asked why some SM30-maintained data appears in customizing transports and some does not. Explained delivery class: the table with config that transported was delivery class C. The table that did not transport was delivery class A. Consultant immediately identified two other tables that were config but marked A — requested developer to change to C and re-transport structure. Done before data entry started — no data re-entry needed.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SM30 access dena functional consultant ko for A class table and expecting them to transport config. They will maintain perfectly — but transport will not include the data. Functional consultants often do not know about delivery class — developer must design tables correctly from start.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Verify SM30 + delivery class setup is correct:
" Step 1: Delivery class must be C for config
SELECT SINGLE contflag
  FROM dd02l INTO @DATA(lv_contflag)
  WHERE tabname = 'ZCFG_PAYMENT_TERMS'
    AND as4local = 'A'.
ASSERT lv_contflag = 'C'.  " must be C for config transport

" Step 2: TMG must be generated (check TDDAT)
SELECT SINGLE area
  FROM tddat INTO @DATA(lv_area)
  WHERE area = 'ZCFG_PAYMENT_TERMS'.
ASSERT sy-subrc = 0.  " TMG must exist

" If both pass: SM30 maintenance + transport works correctly

followupAnswerBank.question: How does delivery class G differ from delivery class C?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Delivery class C client-specific customizing ke liye hai — table mein MANDT field hota hai aur data per client isolated hota hai. Same SAP system mein different clients class C tables mein different configuration rakh sakte hain. Yeh most customizing ke liye standard hai. Delivery class G cross-client customizing ke liye hai — no MANDT field, data system mein saare clients ke across shared hota hai. Ek set of data saare clients mein visible hota hai. Class G tab use hota hai jab configuration client ke regardless system-wide identical honi chahiye. G-class tables ke liye transports cross-client workbench transport requests use karte hain, jabki C-class data client-specific customizing transport requests use karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Delivery class C is for client-specific customizing — the table has a MANDT field and data is isolated per client. Different clients in the same SAP system can have different configuration in class C tables. This is the standard for most customizing. Delivery class G is for cross-client customizing — no MANDT field, data is shared across all clients in the system. One set of data is visible in all clients. Class G is used when the configuration must be identical system-wide regardless of client. Transports for G-class tables use cross-client workbench transport requests, while C-class data uses client-specific customizing transport requests.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Architecture understanding: T000 (SAP client management table) is delivery class G — there is only one list of clients in a system, visible and same across all clients. T042 (payment method configuration) is delivery class C — client 100 development system might have test payment methods, client 200 production has real payment methods, isolated per client. Custom table ZCFG_REGION used for regional config that must be the same in all clients — designed as delivery class G with no MANDT field.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Cross-client config table ko C dena with MANDT field. Then different clients can have different 'cross-client' config — defeating the purpose. If configuration must be the same across all clients in the system, it must be delivery class G without MANDT.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Delivery Class C: client-specific config
" SE11: ZCFG_PAYMENT_RULES, Delivery Class: C
" Fields: MANDT (key), ZPAY_CODE (key), ZPAY_LIMIT
" Client 100 config independent from Client 200 config

" Delivery Class G: cross-client config
" SE11: ZCFG_GLOBAL_SWITCHES, Delivery Class: G
" Fields: ZSWITCH_ID (key), ZSWITCH_VAL (NO MANDT)
" Same data visible in ALL clients of the system

" Check in ABAP: G class table - no client filter:
SELECT SINGLE zswitch_val
  FROM zcfg_global_switches  " No MANDT filter - cross client
  INTO @DATA(lv_switch)
  WHERE zswitch_id = 'FEATURE_X'.

data-dictionary-026.easyUnderstanding:
Application Table samjho ek busy restaurant ke order register ki tarah. Har din customers aate hain, orders dete hain — waiter us register mein likhta hai Table 5 ne Biryani order kiya, Table 3 ne Dal Tadka. Ye sab business transactions hain — daily business operations ka record.

Bilkul aisi tarah SAP mein Application Table wo tables hain jo daily business operations ka data store karti hain. Jab aap sales order create karte ho — VBAK table mein ek nayi row aa jaati hai. Jab material receive hota hai — MKPF table update hoti hai. Jab invoice post hoti hai — BKPF table mein entry hoti hai. Ye sab Application Tables hain — delivery class A ke saath.

In contrast, ek restaurant ka menu card alag cheez hai — wo ek configuration document hai jo rarely change hota hai aur sab waiters ke liye same hota hai. SAP mein ye Customizing Tables hain (delivery class C) — payment terms, document types, tax codes — configuration jo system ka behavior define karti hai.

Application Table ki key characteristics:
- Delivery Class A hoti hai SE11 mein
- Business users aur programs daily enter karte hain data
- Data har system mein alag hota hai — production mein real orders, QA mein test orders
- Transport nahi hota — real business data system-specific rehta hai
- Time ke saath grow karta rehta hai — zyada orders, zyada records
data-dictionary-026.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Application Table ko senior ABAP developer ki tarah samjho:

**Definition:**
- Delivery Class A table = Application Table
- Business transaction data aur business master data store karta hai
- Users aur automated programs daily create aur maintain karte hain data
- Enterprise ke core business operations ka digital record

**Application Tables ke Types:**

**Transactional Application Tables:**
- Sales Orders: VBAK (header), VBAP (items), VBEP (schedule lines)
- Purchase Orders: EKKO (header), EKPO (items)
- Financial Postings: BKPF (accounting doc header), BSEG (line items)
- Material Documents: MKPF (header), MSEG (items)
- Delivery Documents: LIKP (header), LIPS (items)

**Master Data Application Tables:**
- Customer Master: KNA1 (general), KNVV (sales area data)
- Vendor/Supplier Master: LFA1 (general), LFM1 (purchasing)
- Material Master: MARA (general), MARC (plant data), MARD (storage location)
- Asset Master: ANLA (general), ANLB (depreciation)
- Employee Master: PA0001, PA0002 (HR infotypes)

**Characteristics of Application Tables:**
- Delivery Class: A (mandatory for application tables)
- MANDT field: always present (client-specific)
- Size: can grow to millions/billions of rows in large systems
- Not transported: data stays per system
- Backed up: included in system backups
- Archived: old data can be archived via SAP archiving (SARA transaction)

**Custom Application Tables (Z tables):**
- ZCUSTOMER_CONTRACTS: custom business agreements
- ZINVOICE_REGISTER: custom invoice tracking
- ZORDER_TRACKING: custom shipment tracking
- Same characteristics as SAP standard application tables
- Created in SE11 with delivery class A

**Application Table vs Customizing Table — Key Differences:**
| Aspect | Application (A) | Customizing (C) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Data type | Business transactions | System configuration |
| Who maintains | Users/programs | Consultants/IMG |
| Transport | Not transported | Transported |
| Size | Grows with business | Relatively stable |
| Example | VBAK (sales orders) | T042 (payment methods) |
| Custom example | ZCUSTOM_ORDERS | ZCFG_PAYMENT_TYPES |

**Performance Considerations for Application Tables:**
- Large application tables need proper indexing strategy
- Archive old data with SARA to control table growth
- HANA column store handles large application tables well
- Avoid full table scans — always use key fields or indexed fields in WHERE clause

**S/4HANA Impact on Application Tables:**
- ACDOCA replaces multiple FI application tables (BSEG, GLT0, FAGLFLEXA)
- HANA column store improves query performance on large application tables
- Archiving still important — HANA is fast but not unlimited
- New custom application tables still created same way — SE11, delivery class A
data-dictionary-026.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'An Application Table in SAP is a table with delivery class A — it stores business transaction data and master data that is created and maintained through daily business operations. Examples include VBAK for sales order headers, BKPF for accounting document headers, MARA for material master data, and KNA1 for customer master data. These tables grow as the business operates — every new sales order, every goods movement, every financial posting adds records.

The key distinction from Customizing tables: application data is system-specific and not transported between systems. Production sales orders should not transport to QA, and QA test orders should not be in production. This is controlled by delivery class A — customizing transports do not include A-class table data.

When creating custom tables for business process data — like a custom delivery tracking table or a custom contract management table — we set delivery class A in SE11. The design principle: if ABAP programs or business users will be creating records as part of business operations, the table is an Application Table with delivery class A.

In S/4HANA, the most significant change to application tables is ACDOCA — the Universal Journal which consolidates multiple FI application tables into one. New custom application tables are still created the same way in SE11.'

Hinglish tip: Interview mein ek simple analogy use karo: 'Application table = business ka daily ledger. Customizing table = business rules ki book.' Phir ek SAP example aur ek custom Z table example do. Isse pata chalta hai ki aap sirf definition nahi jaante — practical application bhi samajhte ho.
data-dictionary-026.codeExamples:
* Creating and working with application tables
* Demonstrating application data access patterns

" SE11: Create custom application table ZCUSTOM_CONTRACTS
" Delivery Class: A (Application - business data)
" MANDT    CLNT  3    KEY - Client
" ZCONTR_ID NUMC 10   KEY - Contract ID
" ZCUST_ID  CHAR 10       - Customer ID (FK -> KNA1)
" ZSTART_DT DATS  8       - Contract Start Date
" ZEND_DT   DATS  8       - Contract End Date
" ZVALUE    CURR 15,2     - Contract Value
" ZCURR     CUKY  5       - Currency key
" ZSTATUS   CHAR  1       - Status (A=Active, C=Closed)
" CREATED   DATS  8       - Creation Date
" CREATEDBY CHAR 12       - Created By User

" Insert application data (from ABAP program):
DATA(ls_contract) = VALUE zcustom_contracts(
  mandt     = sy-mandt
  zcontr_id = lv_new_id
  zcust_id  = lv_customer
  zstart_dt = sy-datum
  zvalue    = lv_value
  zstatus   = 'A'
  created   = sy-datum
  createdby = sy-uname ).
INSERT INTO zcustom_contracts VALUES @ls_contract.
COMMIT WORK.
data-dictionary-026.interviewScriptEnglish:
An Application Table in SAP is a table with delivery class A — it stores business transaction data and master data that is created and maintained through daily business operations. Examples include VBAK for sales order headers, BKPF for accounting document headers, MARA for material master data, and KNA1 for customer master data. These tables grow as the business operates — every new sales order, every goods movement, every financial posting adds records.

The key distinction from Customizing tables: application data is system-specific and not transported between systems. Production sales orders should not transport to QA, and QA test orders should not be in production. This is controlled by delivery class A — customizing transports do not include A-class table data.

When creating custom tables for business process data — like a custom delivery tracking table or a custom contract management table — we set delivery class A in SE11. The design principle: if ABAP programs or business users will be creating records as part of business operations, the table is an Application Table with delivery class A.

In S/4HANA, the most significant change to application tables is ACDOCA — the Universal Journal which consolidates multiple FI application tables into one. New custom application tables are still created the same way in SE11.
data-dictionary-026.interviewScriptHinglish:
SAP mein Application Table woh table hai jiski delivery class A hoti hai — yeh business transaction data aur master data store karti hai jo daily business operations ke through create aur maintain hota hai. Examples include sales order headers ke liye VBAK, accounting document headers ke liye BKPF, material master data ke liye MARA, aur customer master data ke liye KNA1. Yeh tables business ke operate karne ke saath grow hoti hain — har naya sales order, har goods movement, har financial posting records add karta hai.

Customizing tables se key distinction: application data system-specific hai aur systems ke beech transport nahi hota. Production sales orders QA mein transport nahi hone chahiye, aur QA test orders production mein nahi hone chahiye. Yeh delivery class A se control hota hai — customizing transports A-class table data include nahi karte.

Jab hum business process data ke liye custom tables create karte hain — jaise ek custom delivery tracking table ya ek custom contract management table — hum SE11 mein delivery class A set karte hain. Design principle: agar ABAP programs ya business users business operations ke part ke roop mein records create karenge, toh table Application Table hai delivery class A ke saath.

S/4HANA mein, application tables ke liye sabse significant change ACDOCA hai — Universal Journal jo multiple FI application tables ko ek mein consolidate karta hai. New custom application tables ab bhi SE11 mein usi tarah create hote hain.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Galti: Application table mein dono transactional aur config data mix karna.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Example: VBAK mein ek custom field add karna jo document type configuration store kare — wrong. Config data alag table mein rakho (delivery class C). Application tables mein sirf business transaction data hona chahiye.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Galti: Application table pe SELECT * karna without tight WHERE conditions.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: In development with 100 rows: fast. In production with 50 million rows: timeout/dump. Always code as if the table has 100 million rows — use proper filters from day one.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Galti: Master data table mein transactional data store karna.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Example: customer visit history fields added directly to KNA1 — KNA1 grows unnecessarily, master data becomes bloated. Create a separate transactional table ZCUST_VISIT_LOG (delivery class A) for transactional data referencing KNA1.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Galti: Delivery class bhool ke default chodna aur baad mein problems face karna.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: SE11 mein agar delivery class set nahi kiya — default blank ya something unexpected ho sakta hai. Explicitly A set karo during creation. Dusri galti: MANDT field forget karna — table client-independent ho jaayegi accidentally.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Delivery Class A — stores business transactional data created by end users.
Client field — mandatory first key for client-dependent application tables.
Numeric character key length 10 — typical for document number sequences.
Foreign key relationship to customer master KNA1; DDIC-checked at INSERT.
Currency data type 15 digits 2 decimals — needs paired CUKY field.
Currency key data type length 5 — references TCURC; partner field to CURR.
Inline structure constructor (7.40+ syntax) — fills work area in one statement.
Open SQL INSERT single row; sy-subrc=0 success.
Persists database LUW changes; required after INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on app tables.
Domain-style status flag — Active/Closed; could use domain fixed values for safety.

data-dictionary-026.quickRevisionNotes:
What: An Application Table holds business transactional data — delivery class A, client-dependent with MANDT first key, populated through user transactions or ABAP programs.

Used for:
- Storing custom business data (contracts, orders, custom documents)
- Pairing CURR and CUKY fields for currency amounts
- Foreign-key linking to standard SAP master tables (KNA1, MARA)

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SE11 with Delivery Class A; MANDT mandatory first key
- CURR + CUKY paired fields; FK relationships defined in SE11 Currency/Quantity tab
- INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE + COMMIT WORK; CL_SALV_TABLE for display

Interview keyword: Class A, MANDT first, CURR needs CUKY.

followupAnswerBank.question: Give examples of SAP standard application tables you use frequently in ABAP development.
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Frequently used SAP standard application tables: Sales area — VBAK (order header), VBAP (order items), VBEP (schedule lines), VBUK/VBUP (status tables). Purchasing area — EKKO (PO header), EKPO (PO items), EKBE (PO history). Financial accounting — BKPF (accounting document header), BSEG (FI line items), BSIS (open items index). Material management — MKPF (material document header), MSEG (material document items), MARD (storage location stock). Master data — KNA1 (customer general), MARA (material general), LFA1 (vendor general). Saari delivery class A hain — transactional ya master business data. S/4HANA mein, ACDOCA primary FI application table ke roop mein BSEG/GLT0 ko replace karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Frequently used SAP standard application tables: Sales area — VBAK (order header), VBAP (order items), VBEP (schedule lines), VBUK/VBUP (status tables). Purchasing area — EKKO (PO header), EKPO (PO items), EKBE (PO history). Financial accounting — BKPF (accounting document header), BSEG (FI line items), BSIS (open items index). Material management — MKPF (material document header), MSEG (material document items), MARD (storage location stock). Master data — KNA1 (customer general), MARA (material general), LFA1 (vendor general). All are delivery class A — transactional or master business data. In S/4HANA, ACDOCA replaces BSEG/GLT0 as the primary FI application table.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Daily development pattern: Most business requirements involve reading from 2-3 application tables joined together. Sales invoice report: join VBAK + VBAP + BKPF. Stock overview: join MARA + MARC + MARD. Vendor payment history: join LFA1 + BKPF + BSEG. Knowing these application table relationships is fundamental to ABAP development — you spend more time with application tables than any other table type.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Application table mein dono transactional aur config data mix karna. Example: VBAK mein ek custom field add karna jo document type configuration store kare — wrong. Config data alag table mein rakho (delivery class C). Application tables mein sirf business transaction data hona chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Common application table query patterns:
" Sales order with customer details:
SELECT a~vbeln, a~kunnr, k~name1, p~matnr, p~netwr
  FROM vbak AS a
  INNER JOIN vbap AS p ON a~vbeln = p~vbeln
  INNER JOIN kna1 AS k ON a~kunnr = k~kunnr
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
  WHERE a~erdat = @sy-datum
    AND a~vkorg = @lv_sales_org.

" Material stock per storage location:
SELECT r~matnr, r~maktx, d~werks, d~lgort, d~labst
  FROM mara AS r
  INNER JOIN mard AS d ON r~matnr = d~matnr
  INNER JOIN makt AS t ON r~matnr = t~matnr
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_stock)
  WHERE d~werks = @lv_plant
    AND d~labst > 0.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle large application tables in ABAP — performance best practices?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Badi application tables ke liye performance best practices: WHERE clause mein hamesha key fields ya indexed fields ke through filter karo — million-row tables par full table scans bahut slow hain. Sirf needed fields select karo, SELECT * nahi. Badi result sets ke liye, ek baar mein millions of rows memory mein load avoid karne ke liye SELECT loop mein PACKAGE SIZE use karo. Modern ABAP mein better performance ke liye SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES ki jagah proper JOINs use karo. HANA column store wala S/4HANA mein, aggregation queries (SUM, COUNT, GROUP BY) very fast hain — aggregation database par push karo, ABAP par nahi. Hamesha realistic data volumes ke saath ST05 SQL trace use karke test karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Performance best practices for large application tables: Always filter by key fields or indexed fields in WHERE clause — full table scans on million-row tables are very slow. Select only needed fields, not SELECT *. For large result sets, use PACKAGE SIZE in SELECT loop to avoid loading millions of rows into memory at once. Use proper JOINs instead of SELECT FOR ALL ENTRIES for better performance in modern ABAP. In S/4HANA with HANA column store, aggregation queries (SUM, COUNT, GROUP BY) are very fast — push aggregation to the database, not ABAP. Always test with realistic data volumes using ST05 SQL trace.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance fix: Report reading MSEG (material document items) was timing out. Initial query: SELECT * FROM mseg WHERE matnr = lv_material — no time or plant filter. Table had 50 million rows. Added WHERE werks = lv_plant AND budat BETWEEN lv_from AND lv_to AND matnr = lv_material. Query time: from 180 seconds to 1.2 seconds. Lesson: application tables need multi-field filters on key and indexed fields. One field filter on a 50M row table is always slow.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Application table pe SELECT * karna without tight WHERE conditions. In development with 100 rows: fast. In production with 50 million rows: timeout/dump. Always code as if the table has 100 million rows — use proper filters from day one.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" WRONG: Full table scan on large application table
SELECT * FROM mseg                   " WRONG - no filter
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mseg_all).

" WRONG: Too few filter fields
SELECT * FROM mseg                   " Still risky
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mseg)
  WHERE matnr = @lv_material.

" RIGHT: Multi-field filter + specific columns only
SELECT mblnr, mjahr, zeile, matnr, werks, menge, meins
  FROM mseg
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_movements)
  WHERE werks  = @lv_plant             " plant (indexed)
    AND budat  BETWEEN @lv_from        " date range
               AND @lv_to
    AND matnr  = @lv_material.         " material

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between master data tables and transactional data tables — both are application tables?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Dono master data aur transactional tables delivery class A wali application tables hain, but usage pattern aur growth rate mein differ karti hain. Master data tables business entities store karti hain jo ek baar create hoti hain aur kayi baar referenced hoti hain: customer master ke liye KNA1, material master ke liye MARA, vendor master ke liye LFA1. Yeh slowly grow karti hain — ek company ke shayad 50,000 customers ho jo barely change karte hain. Transactional tables business events store karti hain: sales orders ke liye VBAK, accounting documents ke liye BKPF, material movements ke liye MKPF. Yeh continuously grow karti hain — har din thousands of new records, per year potentially millions. Transactional tables foreign keys ke through master data tables ko reference karti hain. Transactional tables ke liye archiving critical hai; master data less frequently archive hota hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Both master data and transactional tables are application tables with delivery class A, but they differ in usage pattern and growth rate. Master data tables store business entities that are created once and referenced many times: KNA1 for customer master, MARA for material master, LFA1 for vendor master. These grow slowly — a company might have 50,000 customers that barely change. Transactional tables store business events: VBAK for sales orders, BKPF for accounting documents, MKPF for material movements. These grow continuously — thousands of new records every day, potentially millions per year. Transactional tables reference master data tables through foreign keys. Archiving is critical for transactional tables; master data is archived less frequently.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Table growth analysis: KNA1 (customer master) — 45,000 records, grew by 200 in 2 years (new customers). VBAK (sales order headers) — 8 million records, grew by 1.5 million in 2 years. After 5 years, VBAK would have 15 million+ rows — archiving via SARA became critical. KNA1 no archiving needed. This growth difference shows why the distinction between master and transactional application tables matters for performance and storage planning.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Master data table mein transactional data store karna. Example: customer visit history fields added directly to KNA1 — KNA1 grows unnecessarily, master data becomes bloated. Create a separate transactional table ZCUST_VISIT_LOG (delivery class A) for transactional data referencing KNA1.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Master data table access - by key:
SELECT SINGLE kunnr, name1, land1, ort01
  FROM kna1
  INTO @DATA(ls_customer)
  WHERE kunnr = @lv_customer_id.  " direct key access

" Transactional table - by date range (grows fast):
SELECT vbeln, kunnr, erdat, netwr
  FROM vbak
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_orders)
  WHERE kunnr = @lv_customer_id    " master data FK
    AND erdat BETWEEN @lv_from AND @lv_to  " date filter
  ORDER BY erdat DESCENDING.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you create a custom application table for a new business requirement?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE11 mein custom application table create karne ke steps: Pehle, business requirement clearly define karo — kya data store karna hai, kaun create karta hai, key kya hai. SE11 mein, Z-prefixed table name enter karo, Database Table select karo, Create click karo. Short description enter karo. Delivery Class A par set karo. Enhancement Category 'Can be enhanced (deep)' par set karo. Fields define karo: MANDT (CLNT 3, key field, hamesha first), phir business key fields (NUMC ya CHAR type keys), phir appropriate data elements use karke data fields. Technical Settings set karo — expected size par based Data Class APPL0/1/2, appropriate Size Category. Save aur Activate karo. Agar business users ko SM30 maintenance chahiye: Utilities menu se Table Maintenance Generator run karo. Prompt par transport request mein add karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Steps to create a custom application table in SE11: First, define the business requirement clearly — what data needs to be stored, who creates it, what is the key. In SE11, enter Z-prefixed table name, select Database Table, click Create. Enter short description. Set Delivery Class to A. Set Enhancement Category to 'Can be enhanced (deep).' Define fields: MANDT (CLNT 3, key field, always first), then business key fields (NUMC or CHAR type keys), then data fields using appropriate data elements. Set Technical Settings — Data Class APPL0/1/2 based on expected size, appropriate Size Category. Save and Activate. If business users need SM30 maintenance: run Table Maintenance Generator from Utilities menu. Add to transport request when prompted.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Requirement: Custom table to track customer complaints with status and resolution. Designed: ZCUSTOM_COMPLAINT with fields MANDT, ZCOMP_ID (NUMC 8, key, auto-generated), ZCUST_ID (FK to KNA1), ZCOMP_DATE, ZCOMP_DESC (CHAR 255), ZSTATUS (CHAR 1 with domain ZCOMP_STATUS). Delivery class A (business data, users create complaints). No TMG needed — program handles create/update. Activation successful. Program using MODIFY ZCUSTOM_COMPLAINT populates it. Reports use SELECT on it. Proper application table design.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Delivery class bhool ke default chodna aur baad mein problems face karna. SE11 mein agar delivery class set nahi kiya — default blank ya something unexpected ho sakta hai. Explicitly A set karo during creation. Dusri galti: MANDT field forget karna — table client-independent ho jaayegi accidentally.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" After SE11 table creation (ZCUSTOM_COMPLAINT):
" ABAP program to insert application data:

DATA(lv_new_id) = lv_next_complaint_id.  " get next ID

DATA(ls_complaint) = VALUE zcustom_complaint(
  mandt      = sy-mandt
  zcomp_id   = lv_new_id
  zcust_id   = lv_customer
  zcomp_date = sy-datum
  zcomp_desc = lv_description
  zstatus    = 'O'    " Open
  created_by = sy-uname
  created_at = sy-datum ).

INSERT INTO zcustom_complaint VALUES @ls_complaint.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  COMMIT WORK.
  MESSAGE |Complaint { lv_new_id } created| TYPE 'S'.
ELSE.
  ROLLBACK WORK.
  MESSAGE 'Complaint creation failed' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How is an application table different from a view in terms of data storage?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Application table physically database mein data rows store karta hai — har row apne column values ke saath disk aur HANA memory par persisted hota hai. Jab table se SELECT karte ho, actual stored rows returned hote hain. Tables par INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE kar sakte ho. View virtual object hai — yeh saved query definition hai jo ek ya more tables ko join ya filter karta hai, but data store nahi karta. Jab view se SELECT karte ho, database underlying query ko real application tables ke against execute karta hai aur results return karta hai. Views complex table relationships ke upar convenient read interface provide karte hain but saara actual data underlying application tables mein rehta hai. Database view mein directly INSERT nahi kar sakte.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: An application table physically stores data rows in the database — each row with its column values is persisted on disk and in HANA memory. When you SELECT from a table, actual stored rows are returned. You can INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on tables. A view is a virtual object — it is a saved query definition that joins or filters one or more tables, but stores no data itself. When you SELECT from a view, the database executes the underlying query against the real application tables and returns results. Views provide a convenient read interface over complex table relationships but all actual data resides in the underlying application tables. You cannot INSERT directly into a database view.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Design decision: Report needed customer name alongside sales order data. Option 1: Create a database view joining VBAK + KNA1 — select from view. Option 2: Select from VBAK, then join KNA1. Both valid. View approach: cleaner, reusable, simpler program code. But no data stored in view — all data from VBAK and KNA1 application tables. View just defines the join logic. When VBAK is updated, view automatically shows updated data — view has no cache.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: VIEW se UPDATE/INSERT karne ki koshish karna. Database views read-only hoti hain generally. Maintenance views ke kuch exceptions hain but generally views se data modify nahi hota. Application table directly target karo for DML operations.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Application table: stores data physically
INSERT INTO zcustom_complaint  " OK - table
  VALUES @ls_complaint.

" Database view: read-only window over tables
" View ZCUSTOM_COMP_V joins ZCUSTOM_COMPLAINT + KNA1
SELECT cv~zcomp_id, cv~zcomp_desc, cv~zcust_id,
       cv~zstatus,  kn~name1 AS customer_name
  FROM zcustom_comp_v AS cv  " view - no data stored here
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_display)
  WHERE cv~zstatus = 'O'.
" Data comes from ZCUSTOM_COMPLAINT + KNA1 application tables
" View just defines the join

" Cannot INSERT into view:
" INSERT INTO zcustom_comp_v  " ERROR - not allowed

followupAnswerBank.question: What happens to application table data during system refresh?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: System refresh source system (usually production) se target system (usually QA ya development) tak data copy karta hai. Delivery class A wali saari application tables included hoti hain — production ka actual business data target system mein jo bhi tha use replace karta hai. Consequences: QA mein saara test data lost aur production data se replaced; source se customizing class C tables bhi target configuration overwrite karti hain. Yeh production data volumes ke saath realistic testing ke liye useful hai but do risks create karta hai: pehle, GDPR aur data privacy risk — production se personal customer data ab QA mein hai jahan zyada log access rakhte hain; doosre, test data wipe ho jaata hai aur recreate karna padta hai. Many organizations system refresh ke baad application tables mein personal data anonymize karne ke liye data masking implement karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A system refresh copies data from a source system (usually production) to a target system (usually QA or development). All application tables with delivery class A are included — production's actual business data replaces whatever was in the target system. Consequences: all test data in QA is lost and replaced by production data; customizing class C tables from source also overwrite target configuration. This is useful for realistic testing with production data volumes but creates two risks: first, GDPR and data privacy risk — personal customer data from production is now in QA where more people have access; second, test data is wiped and must be recreated. Many organizations implement data masking after system refresh to anonymize personal data in application tables.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Post-refresh process: After production-to-QA refresh, company's data privacy policy required: all KNA1 (customer) NAME1, STRAS (address), TELF1 (phone) fields to be anonymized. Ran data masking program after refresh — replaced real names with 'TEST_CUST_001', real addresses with 'TEST_ADDR_001'. 500,000 customer records anonymized in 20 minutes. BKPF financial amounts replaced with dummy amounts. Only after masking was QA system opened for team access. Application table data = real business data that needs protection.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: System refresh ke baad GDPR/personal data masking bhool jaana. QA mein production personal data expose ho jaana — production customers ka naam, address, phone number — is a compliance violation. Always have a post-refresh data masking process for application tables containing personal data.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Post-refresh data masking for application tables:
" Anonymize customer names in KNA1 (application table):
UPDATE kna1
  SET name1 = |TEST_CUST_{ kunnr }|
  WHERE mandt = sy-mandt.

" Anonymize addresses:
UPDATE kna1
  SET stras = 'TEST_ADDRESS'
    , telf1 = '000-000-0000'
  WHERE mandt = sy-mandt.

COMMIT WORK.
WRITE: / 'Customer data anonymized for QA system'.

" Application table data is real business data
" Always protect personal data in non-production systems

followupAnswerBank.question: What is data archiving and how does it relate to application tables?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Data archiving woh process hai jo purane application table records ko active database se archive files mein move karta hai while keeping them accessible compliance aur reporting ke liye. SAP ka Archive Development Kit (ADK) aur SARA transaction iss ko handle karte hain. Archiving application tables ke liye critical hai kyunki yeh business operations ke saath indefinitely grow karti hain — 10 saal baad, VBAK mein 100 million rows ho sakti hain, query performance degrade karte hue. Archiving completed, old records ko live table se remove karta hai while preserving them archive files mein. Archived records abhi bhi archive information system ke through padhe ja sakte hain agar legal ya audit purposes ke liye chahiye. Large SAP systems mein saari major application tables ke liye archiving strategy design karni padti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Data archiving is the process of moving old application table records out of the active database into archive files while keeping them accessible for compliance and reporting. SAP's Archive Development Kit (ADK) and SARA transaction handle this. Archiving is critical for application tables because they grow indefinitely with business operations — after 10 years, VBAK might have 100 million rows, degrading query performance. Archiving removes completed, old records from the live table while preserving them in archive files. The archived records can still be read via archive information system if needed for legal or audit purposes. Archiving strategy must be designed for all major application tables in large SAP systems.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance problem: MSEG (material document items) had 200 million rows after 8 years of production. Goods receipt and goods issue queries were slow. Solution: Set up SAP standard archiving object MM_MATBEL for material documents older than 3 years that were fully processed. First archiving run: moved 120 million rows from MSEG to archive files. MSEG went from 200M to 80M rows. Query performance improved 60%. Archive information system configured so warehouse team could still look up old movements if needed.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Application table archiving planning ko ignore karna at project start. After 5 years: table performance issues, emergency archiving needed while system is live. Archiving setup should be part of initial project design — identify which application tables will grow, what retention period is needed, and configure archiving objects early.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check table size growth (application tables):
SELECT tabname, nrrows
  FROM dd09l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_sizes)
  WHERE tabname IN ('VBAK', 'BKPF', 'MSEG', 'MKPF')
  ORDER BY nrrows DESCENDING.
" Large nrrows values indicate archiving candidates

" ABAP: check archivability of a document:
" (before archiving, SAP checks document is complete)
CALL FUNCTION 'ARCHIVE_CHECK_OBJECT'
  EXPORTING
    object    = 'SD_VBAK'  " archiving object name
    vbeln     = lv_order_no
  EXCEPTIONS
    not_found = 1.
" sy-subrc = 0: can be archived
" sy-subrc = 1: not yet archivable (open items etc.)

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you identify whether a table is an application table or customizing table when you encounter it?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Table application ya customizing hai yeh identify karne ke liye: Most direct method — SE11 mein table open karo aur Delivery Class field check karo: A = application, C = customizing, G = cross-client customizing. Hints ke roop mein naming conventions (definitive nahi): T se start hone wali tables (T001, T042, TVAK) often customizing hoti hain; VBAK, BKPF, MKPF jaisi transaction tables application hoti hain. Logical analysis: table store karti hai kya hua (business events) ya system ko kaise behave karna chahiye (rules aur configuration)? SE16N data volume check: thousands se millions rows daily change application suggest karta hai; few hundred stable rows customizing suggest karte hain. Programmatically, CONTFLAG field ke liye DD02L table query karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To identify whether a table is application or customizing: Most direct method — open the table in SE11 and check the Delivery Class field: A = application, C = customizing, G = cross-client customizing. Naming conventions as hints (not definitive): tables starting with T (T001, T042, TVAK) are often customizing; transaction tables like VBAK, BKPF, MKPF are application. Logical analysis: does the table store what happened (business events) or how the system should behave (rules and configuration)? SE16N data volume check: thousands to millions of rows changing daily suggests application; few hundred stable rows suggests customizing. Programmatically, query DD02L table for CONTFLAG field.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Investigation: Found unknown table ZTMP_SHIPDATA in system. Need to understand its purpose before modifying. SE11: delivery class A, MANDT present, created date 3 years ago. SE16N: 450,000 rows, all from 3 years ago — not growing. SE15 WHERE-USED: used in one program ZSHIP_MIGRATION_REPORT. Conclusion: A-class application table used in a one-time data migration — data is 3 years old, program no longer runs. Safe to archive old data. Investigation took 5 minutes using SE11, SE16N, SE15.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Table delivery class assume karna based on naming alone. 'ZCONFIG_TABLE' naam is misleading — check actual SE11 delivery class. Some developers name config tables with delivery class A accidentally. Always verify SE11 delivery class, never assume from name.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Programmatic identification of table type:
SELECT tabname, contflag, tabclass
  FROM dd02l
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_table_info)
  WHERE tabname = 'VBAK'
    AND as4local = 'A'.

" Result for VBAK: contflag = 'A' (Application table)
" Result for T042: contflag = 'C' (Customizing table)
" Result for T000: contflag = 'G' (Cross-client customizing)

" Classify custom Z tables:
SELECT tabname,
  CASE contflag
    WHEN 'A' THEN 'Application'
    WHEN 'C' THEN 'Customizing'
    WHEN 'G' THEN 'Cross-client Config'
    WHEN 'L' THEN 'Temporary'
    ELSE contflag
  END AS table_type
  FROM dd02l
  WHERE tabname LIKE 'Z%'
    AND as4local = 'A'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_classified).
